title: Phase 4: Add regression coverage and early validation for exact-share semantics
description:
## Why
This epic needs regression coverage that protects both the documented current math and the new semantics. Without that, solver logic, CAP reporting, and workspace terminology can drift again.

## What needs to be done
Add the highest-leverage regression tests and any early validation that is appropriate after the control-model cleanup.

Required coverage includes:
- solver-reported effective caps and CAP-plot effective caps agree for the same scenario/output/year
- exact one-pathway control works through one-hot exact shares
- dependency expansion and run-status logic reflect active pathways under exact controls
- zero available pathways remains the only demand-blocking availability case
- malformed exact-share controls are caught early if the implementation introduces pre-solve validation for them

## Oracle recommendation for this phase
Favor a small set of high-value tests at the boundaries where semantics can drift: solver reporting, CAP reporting, workspace/run-status presentation, and exact-share control validation.
design:
## Expected focus areas
- web/test/lpAdapter.test.mjs
- web/test/chartData.test.mjs
- web/test/outputRunStatus.test.mjs
- workspace/configuration tests that cover exact-share controls

## Constraints
- Tests should lock in the explicit decision that exact controls do not redefine cap normalization.
acceptance_criteria:
- Regression tests prove solver effective caps and CAP-plot effective caps agree for the same scenario/output/year.
- Tests cover one-hot exact-share control, active-pathway derivation, and zero-available demand blocking.
- Any pre-solve exact-share validation added by the implementation is covered by tests.
- Tests lock in the decision that exact controls do not change cap normalization.

title: Phase 3: Align workspace and CAP wording with available-vs-active semantics
description:
## Why
The solver and CAP plot are already aligned, but the UI still uses wording that implies a different model. That wording is what makes the behavior look buggy even when the math is consistent.

## What needs to be done
Update the workspace and result presentation to use the same semantics established in Phase 1 and the same control model established in Phase 2.

This includes:
- right-sidebar badges and detail copy
- workspace helper text around exact-share controls and pathway availability
- CAP-plot notes and labels so they describe the current denominator accurately
- zero-available-pathway messaging so active demand is blocked only in the true zero-available case

## Oracle recommendation for this phase
Do not present non-disabled pathways as though they are always solver-active. Where useful, show both available and active counts. Keep CAP wording explicit that max-share normalization still runs over available/non-disabled pathways.
design:
## Constraints
- Keep the underlying cap math unchanged.
- Remove ambiguous uses of enabled when the UI actually means available.
- Preserve the product rule that the only special invalid demand case is zero available pathways.
acceptance_criteria:
- Workspace and results UI stop using misleading enabled wording where available and active differ.
- CAP notes/labels describe the denominator as available/non-disabled pathways.
- Zero available pathways remains the only availability state that blocks active demand.

title: Phase 2: Remove pinned_single and converge on exact-share controls
description:
## Why
pinned_single is the main source of the separate mental model that this epic is trying to remove. We no longer need to preserve it as a special concept.

## What needs to be done
Remove pinned_single as a first-class control mode and represent exact one-pathway control as a one-hot exact-share / exact-mix case.

This includes:
- workspace control reconciliation
- configuration/schema/app-config references
- any control-mode-specific solver/request handling that only exists for pinned_single
- checked-in example configurations and tests that still rely on pinned_single

## Oracle recommendation for this phase
Do not carry a long-term compatibility layer here. The simpler and safer direction is to converge on a single exact-share model, with one-pathway exact control represented as a one-hot exact-share assignment.
design:
## Constraints
- Do not keep pinned_single as a product-level concept.
- Do not let exact one-pathway control imply a different cap denominator.

## Acceptance intent
After this phase, exact controls should be understandable as one concept: exact shares, including the 100% single-pathway case.
acceptance_criteria:
- pinned_single is removed as a first-class control concept.
- Exact one-pathway control is represented as a one-hot exact-share case.
- Checked-in schemas/configurations/tests stop relying on pinned_single.
- Exact controls still do not redefine the cap denominator.

title: Phase 1: Establish canonical pathway semantics and shared derivation
description:
## Why
The code currently uses "enabled" to mean different things in different places. That makes future fixes brittle and keeps the UI/state model out of sync with solver behavior.

## What needs to be done
Create a canonical pathway-semantics layer that explicitly separates:
- available pathways: non-disabled states
- active pathways: pathways that are actually participating in the solve under the current control
- cap-eligible pathways: the set used for max-share normalization, which stays equal to available pathways for this issue

Use that shared derivation in the solver/workspace codepaths that currently infer their own versions of pathway participation.

## Oracle recommendation for this phase
Start with a semantics/source-of-truth cleanup before removing pinned_single or changing UI wording. This is the highest-leverage step because it prevents the same drift from reappearing in solver scope, workspace state, and chart/reporting code.
design:
## Expected focus areas
- web/src/solver/solveScope.ts
- web/src/data/packageStore.ts
- shared selectors/helpers that derive state availability and solve participation

## Constraints
- Keep cap normalization semantics unchanged in this phase.
- The resulting names should make it obvious when code is talking about available vs active pathways.
acceptance_criteria:
- Shared derivation distinguishes available, active, and cap-eligible pathways.
- Solver/workspace codepaths stop using ambiguous pathway-participation logic.
- Cap normalization remains intentionally based on available/non-disabled pathways in this phase.

title: Epic: Unify pathway semantics across solver, workspace, and CAP views
description:
## Why
Oracle review found that the solver-side max-share normalization and the CAP plot are already aligned today: both use the same normalization math over the same non-disabled pathway set. The real problem is semantic drift across the app.

Today the codebase mixes at least two different concepts under the word enabled:
- available / non-disabled pathways used for cap normalization and much of the workspace state
- solver-active pathways used by exact controls and dependency expansion

That mismatch makes the UI and workspace controls conceptually misleading, especially around pinned_single, even when the solver and charts are internally consistent.

## Documented answer from the audit
- Solver max-share normalization and CAP-plot effective-cap reporting are aligned today.
- We do not need exact controls to redefine the cap-normalization denominator.
- We do not need backward compatibility for the pinned_single concept.
- The product direction is to model exact one-pathway control as an exact-share / exact-mix case, not as a separate mode with its own mental model.

## What needs to be done
Reshape this work as a phased semantics cleanup rather than a cap-math rewrite.

1. Establish one source of truth for pathway semantics, separating:
   - available pathways: non-disabled states
   - active pathways: pathways that participate in the solve under the current control
   - cap-eligible pathways: same as available pathways for this work
2. Remove pinned_single as a first-class control concept and replace it with one-hot exact shares.
3. Update the workspace and CAP-plot presentation so the UI uses the same pathway vocabulary the solver actually uses.
4. Add regression tests and early validation so the solver/CAP alignment and the new exact-control semantics do not drift.

## Phase plan
- simple-msm-ug6.1 — Phase 1: establish canonical pathway semantics and shared derivation helpers
- simple-msm-ug6.1.1 — Phase 2: remove pinned_single and canonicalize exact one-pathway control as one-hot exact shares
- simple-msm-ug6.2 — Phase 3: align UI/status/CAP wording with available-vs-active semantics while keeping the existing cap denominator
- simple-msm-ug6.3 — Phase 4: add regression coverage and early validation around exact-share controls and solver/CAP agreement
design:
## Oracle findings
- web/src/solver/lpAdapter.ts currently normalizes max-share caps across all non-disabled pathways.
- web/src/results/chartData.ts renders CAP values from the solver-reported effectiveMaxShare, so the chart matches solver math today.
- web/src/solver/solveScope.ts uses a narrower solver-active set for dependency expansion under pinned_single and positive-share fixed_shares.
- web/src/components/workspace/rightSidebarStatus.ts and related workspace state still describe non-disabled pathways as enabled, which creates the mental-model mismatch.

## Design decisions for this epic
- Keep the existing cap denominator semantics: exact controls do not redefine max-share normalization.
- Do not preserve pinned_single as a legacy-first concept. Remove it from the product model rather than keeping a compatibility layer.
- Replace ambiguous enabled wording with explicit concepts such as available and active wherever those concepts differ.
- Prefer a small, semantics-first refactor before any deeper solver-behavior changes.

## Non-goals
- Do not reinterpret exact controls as changing effective max-share math.
- Do not add compatibility code to preserve the old pinned_single UX or storage model.
- Do not rewrite max-share normalization itself unless a later issue finds an actual solver/chart mismatch.
acceptance_criteria:
- The issue records the documented answer that solver max-share normalization and CAP-plot effective-cap reporting already align on today's math and denominator.
- The codebase has one explicit source of truth for available vs active pathway semantics.
- pinned_single is removed in favor of exact-share controls without introducing a compatibility layer.
- Workspace and CAP presentation use terminology that matches solver-visible semantics.
- Regression coverage locks in solver/CAP agreement and the decision that exact controls do not redefine cap normalization.

title: Investigate max-share infeasibility and cap-plot mismatch in built-in configurations
description:
## Why
Several built-in configurations appear to behave inconsistently when `respect_max_share` is enabled. Examples reported so far include `agriculture-only`, `industrial-heat-fossil`, and `buildings-externalized`, while `buildings-endogenous` behaves differently. A likely clue is that the CAP plots show multiple pathways as if they are active, even when the run only has one enabled pathway for the affected outputs. There also appears to be a related state-selection bug where some built-in configurations cannot enable additional pathways beyond the initially selected set.

## What needs to be done
- Reproduce the failing cases with `respect_max_share` enabled for at least `agriculture-only`, `industrial-heat-fossil`, and `buildings-externalized`.
- Determine whether the root cause is true infeasibility, a mismatch between enabled pathways and cap plotting, an incorrect denominator/normalization path, or a state-selection/configuration bug.
- Investigate the discrepancy between what the CAP plots show and what the actual run includes.
- Investigate why some built-in configurations appear unable to enable additional pathways in the workspace.
- Document the root cause and identify the corrective code path once the failure mode is understood.

## Acceptance criteria
- The failing configurations can be reproduced reliably with exact steps.
- The investigation identifies whether the issue is in solve feasibility, enabled-pathway state handling, charting, or a combination.
- The issue records the root cause or, if not fully fixed yet, a narrowed technical diagnosis with the next concrete remediation step.
notes:
Investigation result on 2026-04-10:
- `agriculture-only`, `industrial-heat-fossil`, and `buildings-externalized` fail with `solutionStatus: infeasible` when `respect_max_share` is enabled because their built-in controls use `pinned_single` incumbent states at 100% share while the modeled max-share caps for those states fall below 100% in multiple years.
- Example diagnostics include messages like `assigns 100.0% ... above its max share` for the pinned incumbent states.
- Separate UI inconsistency identified and fixed in this issue: the right-sidebar status path was reporting only the pinned state as enabled, while the solve request and cap charts included all non-disabled pathways. That mismatch made the CAP plots and state selector disagree and made re-enabled pathways appear not to change in the UI.
- Remaining follow-up for workspace failure presentation is tracked separately in `simple-msm-mdo`.

title: Show actionable solve failure reports in the configuration workspace
description:
## Why
When a configuration solve fails under stricter solver settings, the workspace currently falls back to empty chart states or otherwise unhelpful output. That makes it hard to tell whether the failure came from infeasibility, conflicting caps, disabled pathways, or another modeling problem.

## What needs to be done
- Capture and surface solver/build failures in the configuration workspace as a first-class error report instead of rendering chart panels with no usable data.
- Ensure the error presentation suppresses the chart grid when no valid result exists.
- Include enough structured detail to diagnose the failure, such as the solver error message and any available infeasibility or constraint clues.
- Make the report readable enough that a model/config debugging pass can start from the UI alone.

## Acceptance criteria
- A failed workspace solve renders a visible error report instead of empty or misleading charts.
- The report includes the underlying failure message and any available diagnostic detail.
- The chart grid is hidden when the solve does not produce a valid result.

title: Default respect_max_share to true and expose it in the UI
description:
## Why
The earlier `abt` fix only corrected one built-in configuration. The actual product direction is broader: `respect_max_share` should default to `true`, existing built-in configurations should stop shipping `false` for this option, and the setting should be exposed in the workspace UI so users can inspect and change it intentionally.

## What needs to be done
- Change configuration/request resolution so `respect_max_share` defaults to `true` when the option is omitted.
- Update built-in configurations and other shipped configuration references that currently set `respect_max_share` to `false` so they align with the new default behavior.
- Add a solver/options section to the left sidebar UI and expose `respect_max_share` there as the first option.
- Ensure the workspace uses the UI value when building solve requests.

## Acceptance criteria
- A configuration that omits `solver_options.respect_max_share` resolves to `true`.
- Shipped built-in configurations no longer default to ignoring max-share caps.
- The left sidebar exposes `respect_max_share` in an options section and the control defaults to enabled.
- Changing the UI control updates the solve behavior and associated workspace messaging consistently.

title: Fix Save overwriting user configs instead of creating duplicates
description: The Save button now enables for dirty user configurations, but saving a loaded user configuration still creates a second entry instead of replacing the existing one. Repro: load the user configuration 'Buildings DLG', change any setting, click Save, and observe two user configurations named 'Buildings DLG'. Expected: Save should overwrite the loaded user configuration in place, preserving a single entry for that configuration id. Save As should remain the path for creating a new configuration.

title: Set respect_max_share to true for built-in buildings-endogenous config
description:
## Why
The built-in `buildings-endogenous` configuration currently ships with `solver_options.respect_max_share = false`, which lets the run ignore pathway max-share caps. For this built-in configuration, that is not the intended behavior.

## What needs to be done
Update the built-in `buildings-endogenous` configuration so `solver_options.respect_max_share` is set to `true`.

## Acceptance criteria
- `web/src/configurations/buildings-endogenous.json` sets `solver_options.respect_max_share` to `true`.
- The built-in workspace run no longer shows max-share caps as ignored for this configuration.

title: Investigate disabled additional electrified pathways in Buildings Only Electricity config
description:
In the Buildings Only Electricity Electrified configuration, the residential and commercial buildings views show one pathway enabled on the right. Using More does not allow either of the additional electrified pathways to be enabled.

Observed behavior:
- One pathway is enabled by default.
- The enabled pathway can be disabled and re-enabled.
- The two additional electrified pathways cannot be enabled from the More menu.

Expected behavior:
- Additional electrified pathways should be selectable when the configuration is loaded, assuming they are intended to be available in this scenario.

Reproduction:
1. Load the Buildings Only Electricity Electrified configuration.
2. Open residential buildings or commercial buildings.
3. Look at the pathways panel on the right.
4. Click More and try to enable the other electrified pathways.
5. Notice the extra pathways cannot be enabled, while the incumbent enabled pathway can still be toggled off and back on.

Investigation target:
- Determine whether the extra pathways are being incorrectly filtered, disabled by scenario/config state, or blocked by UI state management.

title: Fix Save button staying disabled after editing a user configuration
description: The new configuration sidebar shows both Save and Save As, but Save does not become clickable after loading a user-defined configuration and making an edit. Repro: load an existing user configuration from the sidebar, change a control such as demand growth or a commodity mode, and observe that Save remains disabled. Expected: once a loaded user configuration is dirty relative to its saved base, Save should enable and overwrite that same user configuration without requiring Save As.

title: Add explicit Save button for user configurations
description: The configuration sidebar currently only exposes Save as. Add a persistent Save button and keep Save As visible at all times. Save should be disabled when nothing has changed, disabled for dirty built-in configurations, and enabled for dirty user-defined configurations so an existing user configuration can be overwritten without using Save As.

title: Normalize enabled-pathway max shares and expose pathway output/cap charts
description:
## Why
In the buildings-only endogenous-electricity scenario, enabling policy-frontier electricity changes results through 2040 but not in 2045/2050 because the current explorer config disables max-share enforcement. That lets the incumbent electricity pathway take 100% share even when the library says its late-period max_share is near zero. The current workspace charts also hide the actual pathway mix, so users can see commodity consumption and emissions move without seeing which pathway is producing the output.

## What needs to be done
Implement enabled-pathway max-share normalization when max-share enforcement is on. Treat each enabled pathway's max_share as a weight within the enabled set for a given output-year, excluding disabled pathways from the denominator. This should make a single enabled pathway feasible at 100%, preserve relative shares when multiple pathways are enabled, and avoid requiring users to disable max-share enforcement for subset runs.

Also add per-output pathway charts for outputs with selectable pathways, using one chart card per output with a simple mode toggle: absolute pathway output over time and effective max-share over time. Reuse the same reporting grain so the UI can show both actual pathway activity and cap context without adding a separate standalone max-share chart.

## Notes
Expected behavior for the buildings endogenous-electricity case: with incumbent + policy-frontier enabled and max-share enforcement on, 2045 should remain mostly policy-frontier rather than reverting to 100% incumbent, and 2050 should fully exclude incumbent if its raw cap is zero.
design:
- Normalize only within the enabled subset for a given output-year.
- Keep max-share behavior unchanged when respect_max_share is false, but make the UI clearly indicate that caps are ignored in that run.
- Extend SolveStateShareSummary with rawMaxShare and effectiveMaxShare rather than adding a separate reporting table.
- Use the same chart card for pathway output and pathway cap display to avoid chart sprawl.
acceptance_criteria:
- When respect_max_share is true, enabled pathways are normalized to an effective max-share distribution that sums to 100% for each output-year.
- Disabled pathways are excluded from normalization.
- Null max_share values are treated as weight 1.0.
- If all enabled pathways have raw max_share 0, effective max shares fall back to equal shares across enabled pathways.
- Solver feasibility checks, hard max-share constraints, and fixed-share/pinned conflict validation all use effective max shares.
- Solver reporting exposes both rawMaxShare and effectiveMaxShare per state-share row.
- The workspace renders a per-output pathway chart for selectable outputs such as residential building services, commercial building services, and electricity supply.
- Each pathway chart supports an Output view and a Cap view rather than adding a separate dedicated max-share chart.
- The buildings endogenous-electricity regression shows policy-frontier selected in 2030-2040 and dominating 2045/2050 once effective caps are enforced.

title: Move state selector explainer card below state cards in right sidebar
description:
The right sidebar currently renders the state selector explainer card before the actual state selection cards. Update the ordering so the explainer appears at the bottom of that section, after all real state selection cards.

Current behavior:
- The explainer card is the first card in the right sidebar state selector area.

Desired behavior:
- All actual state selection cards render first.
- The state selector explainer card renders last, at the bottom of the right sidebar section.

title: Collapse excluded sectors and sub-sectors in the right sidebar
description:
Why
The right sidebar can become very tall when many sectors or sub-sectors are excluded from a run. Those excluded items are currently greyed out but still consume a lot of vertical space, which makes the active parts of the scenario harder to scan.

What needs to be done
Apply the same collapse pattern we already use for land sequestration and engineered removals when there are no enabled pathways / selected states. At the sector and sub-sector levels in the right sidebar, excluded groups should default to a collapsed presentation instead of staying expanded and greyed out.

Context
The existing show/hide states behavior is a good reference point for the intended interaction. This issue is specifically about carrying that approach upward to sectors and sub-sectors so excluded demand groups take less space while still remaining discoverable.
acceptance_criteria:
- In the right sidebar, excluded sectors do not remain fully expanded by default.
- In the right sidebar, excluded sub-sectors do not remain fully expanded by default.
- The collapsed interaction follows the existing pattern already used for show states / hide states in land sequestration and engineered removals.
- Excluded groups remain discoverable and can still be expanded by the user when needed.
- Active, included groups continue to be visible without regression.

title: Expose commodity supply mode and fixed-share controls in run configuration
description:
## Summary
The run page does not currently give users a way to choose the solve mode for endogenous supply commodities such as electricity, even though the underlying configuration model and solver support `externalized`, `optimize`, and `fixed_shares`.

We need a first-class way to set these commodity controls both in the saved configuration document and in the run UI.

## Problem
Today the run page mainly exposes commodity price selection, which only applies when a commodity is externalized. For commodities that can instead stay inside the model, the user cannot clearly set whether that commodity should be:
- externalized
- optimized across enabled pathways
- fixed to specified pathway shares

That makes it hard to understand or reproduce runs, and it is especially confusing for supply commodities that are also consumed by other pathways.

## Requested change
Add commodity-level control editing for any output/commodity whose allowed control modes include `externalized`, `optimize`, or `fixed_shares`.

This should cover both:
- the configuration document shape and any config editing/import-export path, so these settings can be authored and persisted explicitly
- the run page UI, so users can inspect and change the setting without hand-editing JSON

If `fixed_shares` is selected, the UI must also expose the fixed-share values and explain what the shares apply to.

## Fixed-share semantics to make explicit
When `fixed_shares` is selected, the share should be described as the share of whichever pathways are enabled for that commodity in the current run, not of all pathways in the catalog.

The UI should make that denominator obvious. For example, if only 2 of 3 electricity supply pathways are enabled, the fixed shares should be understood as the shares across those 2 enabled pathways.

## UX questions to resolve in implementation
- Where on the run page should the commodity solve-mode control live so it is discoverable next to commodity pricing without overloading the panel?
- When `fixed_shares` is active, should the share editor appear inline in the commodity panel, in the pathway list, or in a dedicated details area?
- How should disabled pathways be presented while `fixed_shares` is active so the user can see which pathways are in the denominator?

## Notes
This may currently only matter for electricity, but the implementation should work for any commodity/output with those allowed control modes rather than hard-coding a single commodity.
design: The solver and configuration schema already appear to support these control modes, so this is likely an editor/workspace/UI integration task plus validation and explanatory copy. Keep the implementation generic across eligible supply commodities rather than special-casing electricity. Consider relationship and overlap with simple-msm-czl, which handles how externalized supply pathways are visually represented elsewhere in the workspace.
acceptance_criteria:
- Users can choose `externalized`, `optimize`, or `fixed_shares` for supported supply commodities from the run UI.
- Saved configuration documents can persist those commodity control settings, including fixed-share values.
- When `fixed_shares` is selected, the UI lets the user specify the relevant shares and validates or explains the expected total.
- The UI explicitly states that fixed shares apply across the enabled pathways for that commodity in the current run.
- The resulting run state is understandable from the UI without needing to inspect solver internals.

title: Grey out or disable enabled pathways for externalized sub-sectors
description:
## Summary
When a sub-sector dependency is externalized for a run, the UI still shows its auto-included dependency pathways as enabled even though those pathways are not actually part of the solve.

## Current behavior
In Phase 1 Sector State Explorer, electricity supply can be marked as externalized in the left commodity price panel (for example, Electricity externalized at $90/MWh). In that state, the sector panel still shows Electricity supply as an auto-included dependency with 3 enabled pathways:
- Incumbent thermal-heavy grid mix
- Policy frontier grid supply
- Deep-clean firmed grid supply

Those pathways are not used when supply is externalized, so the state panel is misleading.

## Expected behavior
For any sub-sector that is externalized in the current run, its enabled pathways should not appear as active options. The UI should either:
- grey out the pathways to show they are inactive, or
- disable all pathways for that externalized sub-sector

The panel should make it clear that the included input is the externalized commodity price selection, not the listed pathways.

## Reproduction
1. Open Phase 1 Sector State Explorer.
2. Configure a run where Electricity is externalized via the commodity price panel.
3. Inspect the right-side sector panel for Electricity supply.
4. Observe that enabled pathways are still shown despite the run using externalized supply instead.

title: Repair stale web regression tests after solver API drift
description:
## Why
Running the full `web/` test suite while landing issue `1iw.6` exposed unrelated failures caused by stale test expectations rather than the asset/storage migration itself.

## What
Update or remove tests that still depend on removed exports such as `inspectScenarioLpBuild` and `buildComparisonScenarioPlan`, and finish migrating stale assertions that still read `request.scenario` instead of the current `request.configuration` solver contract.

Representative failures seen during verification:
- `web/test/compareAnalysis.test.mjs` imports `buildComparisonScenarioPlan`, which is no longer exported.
- `web/test/solver.test.mjs` imports `inspectScenarioLpBuild`, which is no longer exported.
- `web/test/solveSubsets.test.mjs` still asserts against `request.scenario`.
- Several `web/test/lpAdapter.test.mjs` cases build requests with the old shape and fail inside `lpAdapter.ts`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `npx tsx --test test/*.test.mjs` passes in `web/`
- Tests use the current solver request shape and exported helper surface consistently
notes: Verified on main at 0b897e5 after running npm ci in web and then npx tsx --test test/*.test.mjs. The suite passes 79/79, there are no remaining references to removed helpers (inspectScenarioLpBuild/buildComparisonScenarioPlan), and no request.scenario assertions remain in web/test or web/src.
close_reason: Already resolved on main by 0b897e5; acceptance criteria verified locally in web.

title: Sweep tests, helper names, comments, and contributor docs for scenario terminology
description:
## Why
Oracle found broad leftover `scenario` naming in tests, helper utilities, comments, CLI output, and contributor-facing docs. This should be a final sweep after the core naming and compatibility work is finished so the repo does not thrash on repeated cleanup.

## What
Do the final terminology pass across tests, helper utilities, comments, CLI/ad-hoc tooling output, and contributor docs.

Representative targets include:
- `web/test/*.test.mjs`
- `web/test/solverTestUtils.mjs`
- remaining comments such as `Auto-solve whenever the scenario changes`
- `README.md`
- `web/README.md`
- other non-archival contributor docs that still describe configuration concepts as scenarios
acceptance_criteria:
- Tests, helper names, comments, and non-archival contributor docs align with the final terminology decisions
- Historical or archival docs are only changed if the compatibility/policy issue says they are in scope

title: Rename UI workspace internals from scenario to configuration
description:
## Why
Oracle found lingering `scenario` naming throughout UI internals even where visible product copy is already configuration-first.

## What
Rename app UI internals to align with the shared terminology once the foundational model naming is settled.

Representative targets include:
- `web/src/pages/ScenarioWorkspacePage.tsx`
- `web/src/hooks/useScenarioSolve.ts`
- `web/src/App.tsx`
- `web/src/index.css`
- `web/src/components/workspace/ConfigurationDocumentPanel.tsx`
- `web/src/components/workspace/LeftSidebar.tsx`
- `web/src/components/workspace/RightSidebar.tsx`
- supporting page imports under `web/src/pages/**`

This includes component names, hook names, CSS class names, and UI-layer imports that still expose `scenario` terminology.
acceptance_criteria:
- UI internals no longer expose stale `scenario` naming once the shared model rename is complete
- This work does not race with foundational data-model renames

title: Configuration terminology cleanup after scenario audit
description:
## Why
Oracle’s terminology audit found that the product is already mostly configuration-first in user-facing flows, but the codebase still contains substantial historical `scenario` naming in UI internals, shared data structures, solver contracts, public asset filenames, persistence keys, tests, and contributor docs.

## What
Track the follow-up cleanup needed to make `configuration` the canonical term across the app and codebase while explicitly handling compatibility-sensitive exceptions.

The Oracle review identified two important constraints:
- some remnants are low-risk copy/internal naming cleanups that can be handled directly
- some remnants are compatibility-sensitive and need an explicit migration/compatibility decision before renaming

## Scope
This epic covers issue-only planning and implementation follow-up for:
- terminology policy and compatibility boundaries
- app-owned copy cleanup
- UI/internal rename work
- shared data-model rename work
- solver/compare contract rename work
- asset/storage migration work
- final test/comment/doc sweep
acceptance_criteria:
- All Oracle-recommended cleanup work is captured as child issues under this epic
- Dependencies reflect the work that cannot safely proceed in parallel
- Compatibility-sensitive `scenario` remnants are explicitly separated from straightforward renames
notes: Oracle audit summary: highest-risk remnants are compatibility-sensitive public asset filenames such as `web/public/app_config/reference_scenario*.json` and `web/public/app_config/scenario_schema*.json`, persisted browser draft/storage surfaces such as `simple-msm.scenario-draft.v2` and legacy fields like `baseConfigurationScenario`, and solver/compare contract terminology such as `request.scenario`, `ResolvedScenarioForSolve`, and raw artifact labels like `scenario_lp`. Lower-risk cleanup exists in app-owned copy, UI internals, CSS class names, tests, comments, and contributor docs. The dependency graph intentionally serializes policy -> shared model/solver -> migration -> final sweep so we can avoid breaking compatibility surfaces or thrashing on overlapping renames.

title: Clarify scoped-solve metadata semantics and backfill regression coverage
description:
Why this issue exists
Even after the UI behavior is corrected, the current naming around included outputs still invites users and future code to confuse seed scope with effective solve participation. We also need stable regression coverage for the semantics clarified by this epic.

What needs to be done
Tighten the user-facing and internal terminology around scoped solves, document the distinction between seed scope and effective inclusion, and add regression tests for the buildings-only endogenous electricity case plus other sidebar status edge cases. If a metadata alias or naming cleanup is desirable, keep it backward-compatible with existing configurations.
acceptance_criteria:
- User-facing copy and internal comments distinguish seed scope from effective run inclusion
- Any metadata naming cleanup remains backward-compatible with existing configurations
- Regression tests cover the buildings-only endogenous electricity case, dependency auto-inclusion, and excluded outputs not rendering as disabled
- Regression tests cover the intended sidebar status semantics introduced by the epic

title: Trim configuration center panel to charts only
description: The configuration workspace center panel should show only the four result charts. Remove the configuration document card and the solve/results summary card from the center column while keeping the sidebars intact.

title: Replace scenario-specific UI and routes with a configuration-first workflow
description:
## Context
The product surface still exposes a separate Scenario page and scenario-specific language even though the intended UX is configuration-first.

## Task
Replace scenario-specific UI, routes, copy, and import/export flows with a single configuration-first workflow. The user should experience one working document concept throughout the app.

## Deliverables
- [ ] Scenario page and route are renamed or replaced with configuration-first equivalents
- [ ] Import/export and draft persistence UX operate on the canonical configuration document
- [ ] Primary UI copy and navigation no longer present scenario/configuration as separate user-facing concepts
- [ ] Stale scenario-specific workflow affordances are removed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A user can load, edit, save, import, and export configurations without encountering a separate scenario workflow
- [ ] App navigation and page copy consistently use configuration-first terminology where they refer to the working document
- [ ] No legacy scenario route or UI is retained purely for backward compatibility

## Dependencies
Requires the canonical model task and the unified store/execution lifecycle.

title: Add regression coverage and docs for the single-configuration architecture
description:
## Context
The refactor changes core architecture, terminology, and persistence semantics. Tests and docs need to verify the single-model behavior and remove stale references to the old split.

## Task
Add or update high-value regression coverage for the canonical configuration model and rewrite repository documentation to describe one configuration-first architecture.

## Deliverables
- [ ] Regression coverage for canonical configuration load/save/solve behavior
- [ ] Coverage for built-in configuration loading without overlay inheritance
- [ ] README and other relevant docs updated to describe one configuration model
- [ ] Stale docs and comments about separate scenario/configuration concepts removed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tests fail if a hidden overlay/reference merge path is reintroduced
- [ ] Documentation explains one canonical configuration model and one user-facing working document concept
- [ ] No stale repository guidance instructs contributors to preserve the old split

## Dependencies
Requires the built-in configuration task, the unified store/execution task, and the configuration-first UI task.

title: Unify store, solve, and compare flows on current configuration
description:
## Context
`packageStore`, solve request building, results, and compare mode currently operate on `currentScenario` plus separate configuration metadata. After convergence, the app should have one active configuration lifecycle.

## Task
Refactor state management and execution paths so workspace state, compare mode, and solver preparation all run from one canonical `currentConfiguration` object. Remove store and execution semantics that only exist because of the scenario/configuration split.

## Deliverables
- [ ] Store state and actions use one active configuration lifecycle
- [ ] Solve request building consumes the canonical configuration directly
- [ ] Results and Compare derive from the same current configuration object used by the workspace
- [ ] Dirty-state and saved-config tracking work without parallel scenario/configuration bookkeeping

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The app no longer needs separate `currentScenario` and configuration overlay semantics to build solves
- [ ] Compare and Results use the same canonical configuration object as the workspace editor
- [ ] Removed lifecycle state is deleted rather than retained behind compatibility aliases

## Dependencies
Requires the canonical model task and the full built-in configuration loading task.

title: Materialize built-in configurations as full documents
description:
## Context
Built-in configurations under `web/src/configurations/` are currently overlays that materialize on top of `reference_scenario.json`. The single-model architecture requires built-ins to be full configuration documents in their own right.

## Task
Convert built-in configuration assets and their loaders so each built-in entry is a complete configuration document. Remove runtime reference-overlay materialization for built-ins.

## Deliverables
- [ ] Built-in configuration JSON files represent complete canonical configuration documents
- [ ] Loader code reads full configurations directly
- [ ] Reference configuration is represented as a normal built-in configuration, not as a special runtime merge base
- [ ] Dead overlay-loading code is removed

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Loading a built-in configuration does not depend on applying an overlay to a separate reference scenario
- [ ] The built-in configuration set can be solved, persisted, and exported without hidden inheritance
- [ ] No legacy overlay format is kept around for compatibility

## Dependencies
Requires the canonical configuration document from the core model task.

title: Define the canonical configuration document and remove overlay types
description:
## Context
The app currently has two overlapping domain concepts: a full `ScenarioDocument` used for solving and persistence, and a `SolveConfiguration` overlay merged onto a reference scenario. The target state is one canonical full configuration document with no legacy compatibility layer.

## Task
Define and implement the single canonical configuration document at the type/model layer. Remove the overlay-style configuration concept from the core domain model and make the surviving type the source of truth for all app workflows.

## Deliverables
- [ ] Canonical full configuration type defined in the core data model
- [ ] Output scope persistence is represented in the canonical configuration model or its explicit app metadata
- [ ] Overlay-specific types/helpers are removed or renamed out of the product surface
- [ ] Core naming uses configuration-first terminology instead of scenario/configuration split terminology

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] There is one canonical full configuration document shape that can express the active app state without layering onto another document
- [ ] No new backward-compatibility shim is introduced for legacy scenario JSON or overlay configs
- [ ] The type/model layer no longer treats scenario and configuration as separate peer concepts

## Dependencies
None

title: Converge on a single configuration model across the app
description:
## Objective
Converge the web app on one canonical configuration document and remove the separate scenario/configuration split.

This work should leave the repository with a single full configuration model used by built-in presets, browser drafts, import/export, workspace state, compare mode, and solver preparation. Do not preserve backward compatibility for legacy scenario JSON or overlay-style configuration formats; replace them.

## Scope
In scope:
- Canonicalize one full configuration document shape
- Remove overlay/reference-merge loading paths
- Rename store/UI/docs concepts to configuration-first language
- Ensure built-in configurations, browser drafts, compare mode, and solver flows all use the same persisted model

Out of scope:
- Adding migration shims for old scenario/configuration formats
- Maintaining legacy routes or legacy import/export behavior beyond what is needed to finish the refactor

## Subtask Verification Checklist
- [ ] **simple-msm-tnd.1**: Core type/model layer defines one canonical configuration document and removes the overlay split
- [ ] **simple-msm-tnd.2**: Built-in configuration files and loaders use full documents directly, without reference overlay materialization
- [ ] **simple-msm-tnd.3**: Store, solve, results, and compare flows use one `currentConfiguration` lifecycle
- [ ] **simple-msm-tnd.4**: UI routes, pages, and import/export flows are configuration-first and no longer present a separate scenario workflow
- [ ] **simple-msm-tnd.5**: Tests and docs cover the new architecture and remove stale split-language

## Integration Testing
Run these commands from `web/`:

```bash
npm run lint
npm run build
npx tsx --test test/*.test.mjs
```

## End-to-End Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The app has one user-facing working document concept: configuration
- [ ] Built-in configurations load without merging onto a separate reference scenario object at runtime
- [ ] Import/export/draft persistence use the canonical configuration document only
- [ ] Results and Compare run from the same current configuration object used by the workspace UI
- [ ] Repository docs describe a single configuration model and do not present scenario/configuration as peer concepts

title: User-defined configurations: repo-persisted, separate from test fixtures
description:
Currently user configurations are stored in localStorage (configurationLoader.ts). We need to support saving user-defined configurations to the repository so they persist across machines and are shareable, while keeping them clearly separated from the built-in test configurations.

Requirements:
1. User-defined configs should be saved to a separate directory in the repo (e.g. configurations/user/ vs configurations/ for built-ins)
2. The configuration selector UI should clearly distinguish:
   - Built-in/test configs: read-only, labeled as 'test' or 'built-in', cannot be overwritten
   - User-defined configs: editable, can be loaded, modified, and re-saved (overwrite) or saved as new
3. User configs should support 'Save' (overwrite existing) and 'Save As' (create new) workflows
4. Loading a user config, modifying it, then saving should update the existing config in-place
5. Need a mechanism to write configs back to the repo (could be a local file write via dev server endpoint, or export/import workflow)

Files involved:
- web/src/data/configurationLoader.ts — add repo-based user config loading/saving
- web/src/pages/ConfigurationsPage.tsx — update UI for save/save-as, distinguish config types
- Possibly a small API endpoint or build-time convention for writing user configs to disk

title: State selector: allow disabling all states in a subsector to turn it off
description:
Currently in the state selector (RightSidebar), the toggleStateEnabled logic in packageStore prevents the user from disabling the last remaining state (returns early when enabledIds.length === 0 at line 150). We should allow all states to be turned off, which effectively disables the entire subsector (zero demand).

When all states in a subsector are disabled:
- The subsector should visually collapse its state chip list
- Show a 'disabled' indicator (badge or icon) on the subsector header
- Require a click on the subsector header or indicator to re-expand and re-select states
- Any subsector with nothing selected should show as disabled

Files involved:
- web/src/data/packageStore.ts (toggleStateEnabled — remove the early return, set mode to 'off' when all disabled)
- web/src/components/workspace/RightSidebar.tsx (collapse UI, disabled indicator, click-to-expand)
- CSS for disabled/collapsed subsector styling

title: Deselect active configuration when state selection is modified
description:
When a user loads an existing configuration (built-in or user), it becomes the activeConfigurationId in the store. If the user then modifies state selections via the state selector, the scenario no longer matches that configuration, but the UI still shows it as 'active'.

The fix: when toggleStateEnabled (or any state-mutating action) modifies service_controls, clear activeConfigurationId so the configuration selector no longer highlights the previously loaded config.

Files involved:
- web/src/data/packageStore.ts — in toggleStateEnabled (and any other scenario-mutating actions), set activeConfigurationId to null when the scenario is modified

title: Refactor commodity pricing to per-commodity low/medium/high selectors and add carbon price driver
description:
## Problem
The current commodity price system uses named bundle presets (central_placeholder, fossil_shock, cheap_clean_energy) that set ALL commodity prices together. Users cannot independently vary individual commodity prices. There is also no carbon/emissions price driver — carbon_price is hardcoded to zero in the reference scenario.

## Goal
1. **Per-commodity low/medium/high selectors** — Replace the single preset picker with independent low/medium/high chips for each commodity (electricity, natural_gas, coal, refined_liquid_fuels, hydrogen, biomass, iron_ore, scrap_steel, sequestration_service). UI should match the state selector pattern in RightSidebar.
2. **Carbon/emissions price driver** — Add a separate 'Emissions Price' section with low/medium/high trajectory presets for carbon price in AUD/tCO2.

## Design

### Data model changes
- **ScenarioCommodityPricing** (types.ts): Replace `preset_id: string` with `selections_by_commodity: Partial<Record<string, PriceLevel>>`. Keep `overrides` for future manual edits. Missing commodities default to 'medium'.
- **commodity_price_presets.json**: Reshape from named bundles to per-commodity registry with `levels: { low, medium, high }`. Seed from existing bundles: low=cheap_clean_energy, medium=central_placeholder, high=fossil_shock.
- **New carbon_price_presets.json**: Three trajectories starting at ~$30/tCO2 in 2025: low→$50 by 2050, medium→$100 by 2050, high→$300 by 2050.
- **AppConfigRegistry**: Add `carbon_price_presets` field. Change `commodity_price_presets` type to per-commodity drivers.
- **reference_scenario.json**: Update commodity_pricing to selections_by_commodity (all 'medium'). Set carbon_price to low trajectory (non-zero).

### Store changes (packageStore.ts)
- Replace `setCommodityPricePreset(presetId)` with `setCommodityPriceLevel(commodityId, level)` and `setCarbonPricePreset(presetId)`.

### Solver changes (buildSolveRequest.ts)
- Resolve commodity prices per-commodity from selections_by_commodity + levels instead of from a single bundle preset.
- Carbon price resolution is unchanged (reads scenario.carbon_price directly).

### UI changes (LeftSidebar.tsx)
- Replace bundle chip row with per-commodity rows (each with low/medium/high chips).
- Add new 'Emissions Price' section with low/medium/high chips.

### Migration
- Map old preset_id values to selections_by_commodity on load: central_placeholder→all medium, cheap_clean_energy→all low, fossil_shock→all high.

### Files to touch
- web/src/data/types.ts (PriceLevel type, ScenarioCommodityPricing, CommodityPriceDriver, CarbonPricePreset, AppConfigRegistry)
- web/src/app_config/commodity_price_presets.json (reshape to per-commodity levels)
- web/src/app_config/carbon_price_presets.json (new)
- web/src/app_config/reference_scenario.json (new commodity_pricing shape, non-zero carbon_price)
- web/src/data/packageStore.ts (new store actions)
- web/src/data/scenarioWorkspaceModel.ts (update getActivePricePreset or replace)
- web/src/data/configurationTypes.ts (commodityPriceSelections, remove commodityPricePresetId)
- web/src/solver/buildSolveRequest.ts (resolve from per-commodity selections)
- web/src/components/workspace/LeftSidebar.tsx (per-commodity chips + emissions section)
- web/src/data/configurationLoader.ts (migration shim for old preset_id)
- web/src/data/packageLoader.ts (load new carbon_price_presets)
- Any compare/scenario pages referencing old preset_id
design: Per-commodity selections_by_commodity replaces single preset_id. Carbon price stays as explicit year table in scenario; presets in app config write into it. Existing bundle values seed low/medium/high directly (cheap_clean=low, central=medium, fossil_shock=high). UI chips pattern matches RightSidebar state selector.
close_reason: Implemented per-commodity low/medium/high price selectors and carbon price driver. All tests pass, build succeeds.

title: Scenario results: 2-column chart grid with new chart types
description:
Redesign the Solve & Results section of ScenarioWorkspacePage to use a 2-column chart grid (2×3) and add three new chart types.

## Current State
Three charts displayed in a single vertical column:
1. Emissions by Sector
2. Commodity Consumption
3. Service Demand Over Time

## Requested Changes

### Layout
- Replace vertical stack with a 2-column responsive grid (desktop: 2×3, mobile: 1 column)
- Use CSS grid: `grid-template-columns: repeat(2, minmax(0, 1fr))`

### Chart Grid (6 charts, 2×3)
Row 1:
1. Service Demand by Output (existing)
2. Demand by Sub-sector (NEW) — aggregate output-level demand up to subsector via output→subsector mapping from solver rows

Row 2:
3. Emissions by Sector (existing)
4. Emissions by Sub-sector (NEW) — same logic as emissions by sector but grouped on `row.subsector`

Row 3:
5. Commodity Consumption (existing)
6. Conversion Cost by Sub-sector (NEW) — `activity × conversionCostPerUnit` grouped by subsector

### New Chart Data Builders (in chartData.ts)
- `buildEmissionsBySubsectorChart(request, result)` — group on `row.subsector`
- `buildDemandBySubsectorChart(request)` — map outputId→subsector via rows, then aggregate demand
- `buildConversionCostBySubsectorChart(request, result)` — `ss.activity * (row.conversionCostPerUnit ?? 0)` grouped by subsector

### Active States Summary
- Add a summary panel below the chart grid showing which states are selected
- Group by output, then by year, list states with share > 0 or activity > 0
- Sort descending by share/activity

### Design Notes
- Reuse existing StackedAreaChart for all 6 charts (no new chart component needed)
- Label cost chart as 'Conversion Cost' not 'Total Cost' since we only have one cost component
- Handle output→subsector mapping carefully: if multiple subsectors exist for one output, fall back to outputLabel
- Sort series by total descending, consider grouping tiny series into 'Other' for readability

title: Investigate endogenous electricity infeasibility in relaxed-cap baseline
description:
## Why this issue exists
Relaxing the packaged reference scenario by disabling `respect_max_share` removes the visible exact-share conflicts, but the LP still returns infeasible unless electricity is switched to `externalized`. That leaves a deeper endogenous-electricity feasibility or diagnosability gap in the current solver path.

## Reproduction
1. Start from the packaged reference scenario.
2. Set `solver_options.respect_max_share` to `false`.
3. Leave electricity in-model with the current fixed-share control.
4. Open `Results`.
5. Observe the LP still returns `infeasible` with no specific electricity-level diagnostic beyond `service_and_supply_lp_not_optimal`.

## Expected behavior
Either the relaxed scenario should solve with endogenous electricity, or the solver should surface a specific pre-solve/diagnostic reason for the electricity infeasibility so the failure is actionable.

## Notes
This was discovered while fixing `simple-msm-1mx`. The current likely first-run fix is to ship a solvable baseline scenario, but the endogenous electricity formulation or diagnostics still need follow-up.

title: 6/7 — Remove dead seed_output_ids / included_output_ids from app_metadata
description:
The app_metadata.seed_output_ids (and its predecessor included_output_ids) field is a holdover from when scope was manually specified. Scope is now derived dynamically from active states in deriveSeedOutputIds(). The field is:
- Defined in full-model-incumbents.json (lists all 11 required-service outputs)
- Read and normalized in configurationLoader.ts (normalizeConfigurationMetadata, normalizeSeedOutputIds)

2. Remove the normalizeSeedOutputIds, getSeedOutputIds, getIncludedOutputIds, withSeedOutputIds, setIncludedOutputIds helpers
3. Remove the seed_output_ids field from the ConfigurationDocument app_metadata type
4. Remove getConfigIncludedOutputIds from solve.mjs
5. Clean up any imports/exports that reference these

Can be done independently.

title: Clean app-owned UI copy and preset text that still says scenario
description:
## Why

title: Rename the Configuration page to Run
description:
## Why
The current top-level page label, `Configuration`, overstates the document-editing aspect and understates that this is the main place where users set up and run the model. The nav already uses short labels (`Compare`, `Library`, `Methods`), so this page should use a short label too.

## What
Rename the user-facing `Configuration` page to `Run` across the app navigation and related user-facing copy where that page is referenced. Treat `Run` as the preferred label unless we discover strong reasons to keep `Configuration`. Do not use `Run Model` unless a later review finds that `Run` is too ambiguous.

Check:
- top navigation label
- page references in README and in-app copy
- any other user-facing references to the page name
- whether internal component/file names should stay as workspace-oriented names or also be aligned

## Deliverable
A consistent user-facing rename from `Configuration` to `Run`, with a clear note on whether internal workspace/component naming should remain as-is or be updated too.
design: Preferred label: 'Run'. Rationale: short, action-oriented, matches the existing single-word nav pattern better than 'Run Model', and better reflects that the page is where configuration and solve happen together.
acceptance_criteria: Top navigation uses 'Run' instead of 'Configuration'; user-facing docs and copy referring to that page are updated consistently; the issue resolves whether internal names remain workspace-oriented or also change

title: Oracle audit: remove all remaining 'scenario' naming in favor of configuration
description:
## Why
We previously chose `configuration` as the canonical term over `scenario`, so the codebase and UI should no longer use `scenario` naming except where it is truly unavoidable historical data or third-party surface area. We need a thorough audit to confirm that decision was fully applied.

## What
Ask Oracle to review the UI and the entire codebase for any remaining `scenario` references, including user-facing copy, component names, routes, props, state, selectors, docs, tests, comments, internal types, API shapes, storage keys, migration leftovers, and any other code paths where the old terminology may still appear.

Oracle should identify:
- every remaining `scenario` reference that should be renamed to `configuration`
- any places where a rename would be risky because of persisted data, compatibility, or external contracts
- any inconsistent mixed terminology that should be cleaned up
- concrete recommendations for follow-up cleanup work

## Deliverable
A review report listing findings, risk notes, and recommended cleanup actions needed to make `configuration` the only term used across the product and codebase where appropriate.
acceptance_criteria:
- Oracle performs a thorough audit of the UI and codebase for `scenario` naming
- The review distinguishes acceptable compatibility exceptions from true cleanup candidates
- The output includes concrete cleanup recommendations or explicitly states that no cleanup is required
- The issue captures enough context that someone can execute the audit without needing extra background from this thread
notes:
Use Oracle for the review rather than a quick grep-only pass. The goal is a thorough terminology audit and cleanup recommendation, not just a string search.
Oracle audit completed. Follow-up epic: simple-msm-1iw. Child issues: simple-msm-1iw.1 (compatibility boundary), simple-msm-1iw.2 (copy cleanup), simple-msm-1iw.3 (shared model rename), simple-msm-1iw.4 (UI internals rename), simple-msm-1iw.5 (solver/compare contract rename), simple-msm-1iw.6 (public asset and persistence migration), simple-msm-1iw.7 (final tests/comments/docs sweep). Dependencies serialize the compatibility-sensitive and shared-contract work so only safe cleanup can proceed in parallel.
close_reason: Oracle review completed and follow-up cleanup epic/issues created

title: Restructure configuration list layout in left sidebar
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done

The Configurations section in the left sidebar is currently rendered as one flat chip list in `web/src/components/workspace/LeftSidebar.tsx`. We want that area to read more like the right sidebar state selector tree, with clear subheadings and a more deliberate layout for the save controls.

Requested scope:
- keep this issue focused on layout and grouping only
- add a top-level Configurations section with two subsections: User configurations and Built-in configurations
- render User configurations above Built-in configurations
- keep the Save and Save as controls directly under the Configurations heading, not inside either subsection
- consider giving Save as a more distinct treatment so it reads differently from Save

Relevant references:
- left sidebar configurations UI: `web/src/components/workspace/LeftSidebar.tsx`
- right sidebar heading pattern to mirror: `web/src/components/workspace/RightSidebar.tsx`
- shared sidebar styling: `web/src/index.css`
design: Prefer reusing the existing sector/subsector heading pattern from the right sidebar instead of inventing a new visual system. Keep behavioral changes out of scope unless needed to support the layout.
acceptance_criteria:
- the left sidebar Configurations area is no longer a single flat list
- the section shows subsection headings for User configurations and Built-in configurations
- user configurations appear before built-in configurations
- Save and Save as are visually grouped directly under the main Configurations heading, outside the two subsections
- the layout change does not change configuration loading, saving, overwriting, or deletion behavior

title: Fix useScenarioSolve lint failure
description:
## Context
`npm run lint` currently fails because `web/src/hooks/useScenarioSolve.ts` calls `solve()` synchronously inside a `useEffect`, which trips the `react-hooks/set-state-in-effect` rule.

## Task
Refactor `useScenarioSolve` so it still autosolves on relevant configuration changes without triggering the lint rule.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `npm run lint` passes for the hook change
- [ ] Autosolve behavior still works when the current scenario/configuration changes
- [ ] No redundant solve loops or cascading renders are introduced

title: Update demand chart to percentage terms (indexed to 2025=100%)
description: The 'Service Demand Over Time' chart should display values in percentage terms, where 100% corresponds to the 2025 value. Currently it shows a line chart with absolute values — change the y-axis to show indexed percentages instead.

title: Match single-column chart line thickness to two-column charts
description: The full-width input coefficient chart should keep the same perceived stroke weight and dash spacing as the two-column charts. Update the shared chart rendering so wide-layout charts preserve line and grid thickness consistently.

title: Match single-column chart font sizing to two-column charts
description: The full-width input coefficient chart in the Library page renders axis/legend text larger than the two-column chart cards. Update the chart sizing so the single-column chart typography matches the two-column charts without changing the shared visual treatment elsewhere.

title: Unify Library input coefficient trajectories into a single shared chart
description: The Library page should render input coefficient trajectories as one full-width shared chart, matching the appearance and selector behavior of the other trajectory charts. Replace the per-state coefficient cards with a single chart that plots all states together and uses the same state highlight selection.

title: Adjust Library facet grid to two columns and move input coefficient trajectories higher
description: The Library page currently shows the trajectory facet cards in a three-column layout and places the Input coefficient trajectories card lower than desired. Update the layout to use two columns and position the Input coefficient trajectories card directly below the top set of trajectory cards.

title: Add soft-constraint mode for infeasibility diagnosis
description: Introduce the optional soft-constraint mode described as a v0.2 nice-to-have so analysts can relax constraints and understand near-feasible solutions. Build it on top of the existing diagnostics rather than replacing the hard-constraint path.
acceptance_criteria:
- Users can enable soft constraints explicitly
- Result output identifies the penalties or slacks used to restore feasibility
- Hard-constraint behavior remains the default and primary modeling mode

title: Add scenario save/load and local persistence
description: Implement the first convenience persistence layer after the core workflow is stable: scenario JSON import/export plus browser-local draft persistence. Keep this separate from the critical v1 modeling path.
acceptance_criteria:
- Users can export the current scenario to JSON
- Users can import a scenario JSON file that validates against the app schema
- Local persistence restores the most recent draft without changing core modeling semantics

title: Add code-splitting to reduce 2.4 MB JS bundle
description: Vite build produces a single 2.4 MB JS chunk (385 KB gzipped). Use React.lazy() + dynamic imports for route-level pages (ComparePage, LibraryPage, MethodsPage, ModelFormulationPage, StateSchemaPage) to split the bundle. Acceptance: no single chunk >500 KB, build warning gone.
close_reason: Code-splitting implemented: React.lazy routes, lazy globs, vendor chunks. Max chunk 349 KB, build warning gone.

title: Add CI workflow for build, lint, and test
description: No .github/workflows/ directory exists. Add a basic GitHub Actions workflow that runs bun install, bun run lint, bun run build, and bun run test on push/PR to main. Acceptance: CI runs on PRs and push to main.

title: Review Model Formulation page follow-up items
description: Why: keep space for any documentation or UI follow-up discovered during implementation that should not block the initial page landing. What: hold follow-up items only if they remain after the Model Formulation page is implemented and verified.

title: Support optional enrichment when companion files are present
description: Teach the app to detect and incorporate optional ledgers, schema files, and explanatory docs when they exist, without making them mandatory for startup or core modeling. This should enrich existing views rather than create a separate ingestion path.
acceptance_criteria:
